draft.introduction: Mithraic tauroctony inscriptions (c. 2nd–4th century CE) accompany the central icon of Mithraic worship: the god Mithras slaying a bull. These dedicatory texts from mithraea across the Roman Empire reveal the vocabulary and piety of the Mithraic mysteries.

translation.original_ref: Corpus Inscriptionum et Monumentorum Religionis Mithriacae 423
translation.text: To the unconquered god Mithras, to the unconquered Sun, Gaius Sacidius Barbarus, centurion of the Fifteenth Legion, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
translation.original_text: Deo invicto Mithrae, Soli invicto, C. Sacidius Barbarus, centurio leg(ionis) XV, v(otum) s(olvit) l(ibens) m(erito).

translator_notes.note: The formula “Deo invicto Mithrae” equates Mithras with Sol Invictus, the unconquered Sun. This solar identification made Mithraism Christianity’s closest competitor: both centered on a cosmic savior figure, used communal meals, and promised salvation.
